articles.title: No rain in Delhi for next five days? IMD says temperatures to rise - newsdrum.in
articles.full_text:
New Delhi: Delhi is likely to get a break from the recent spell of heavy monsoon rain, with the India Meteorological Department (IMD) forecasting a gradual rise in daytime temperatures over the next five days and largely dry weather across the national capital.
The IMD said maximum temperatures are likely to increase by 2-3 degrees Celsius during the next five days, with daytime temperatures expected to remain between 37 degrees Celsius and 39 degrees Celsius for most of the coming week.
However, the forecast does not suggest a completely rain-free week.
According to the IMD's seven-day forecast, July 12 and July 13 are expected to remain partly cloudy without any significant rainfall. From July 14 onwards, there is a possibility of very light to light rain accompanied by thunderstorms and lightning during the afternoon or evening, although no widespread or heavy rainfall has been forecast.
The weather department has not issued any warning for Delhi over the next two days, indicating that the city is unlikely to witness any major weather-related disruption in the immediate future.
The dry spell follows two days of widespread rain that brought relief from the humid conditions and caused waterlogging in several parts of the city. With monsoon activity weakening temporarily over Delhi, sunshine is expected to become more prominent and humidity levels may rise again.
At Safdarjung, Delhi's base weather station, the maximum temperature on Saturday settled at 35.7 degrees Celsius, while the minimum was 26.6 degrees Celsius. No rainfall was recorded during the 24 hours ending 8.30 am or during the day until 5.30 pm.
The IMD's district-wise forecast also indicates similar weather across all parts of Delhi, including North, South, East, West, Central and New Delhi districts, with mainly cloudy to partly cloudy skies and only isolated chances of brief light showers later in the week.
While Delhi is expected to remain largely dry, the broader monsoon remains active over several parts of north and east India, with the IMD continuing to forecast heavy rainfall in states including Uttarakhand, Himachal Pradesh, Uttar Pradesh, Bihar and parts of Punjab and Haryana over the coming days.
